payload.formula_text: At Risk Criteria = (R_Score <= 2 AND F_Score >= 4 AND M_Score >= 4) OR (churn_probability > 80%)
payload.formula_explanation: Identifies high-value customers at risk of churning based on RFM scores and predictive churn probability. These are customers who have purchased frequently and spent significantly but have not been active recently. They require immediate attention.
how_to_read: Table shows customers flagged as high churn risk, sorted by lifetime value
Days since last activity shows how long since their last purchase or engagement
Churn Risk percentage is Klaviyos predictive model output
RFM Score shows their R, F, M values concatenated (e.g., 155 = R1, F5, M5)
Red badges indicate critical churn risk (>80%)
Click a customer row to view their full profile and history
decisions_answered: Which high-value customers are most likely to churn?
How much revenue is at risk from churning customers?
Which customers should receive immediate personal outreach?
What is the common pattern among at-risk customers?
payload.narration_text:
This alert table highlights your highest-value customers who are at risk of churning.

The selection criteria: Customers who used to buy frequently with high order values - indicated by F and M scores of 4 or 5 - but have not purchased recently, shown by R scores of 1 or 2. We also include anyone with churn probability above 80%.

Reading the table: Customers are sorted by lifetime value by default - your most valuable at-risk customers appear first. The churn risk percentage comes from Klaviyos predictive analytics. Days since activity shows how long since they last engaged.

This is your action list: These customers represent significant revenue at risk. A $2,000 CLV customer with 90% churn risk needs immediate attention - perhaps a personal email, exclusive offer, or phone call. Export this list for your retention campaign.

payload.formula_text:
Repeat Rate = (Customers with 2+ Orders / Total Cohort Customers) x 100
Avg CLV = SUM(cohort_clv) / COUNT(cohort_customers)
payload.formula_explanation: Groups customers by their first purchase month (cohort) and tracks their repeat purchase behavior. Shows how different acquisition cohorts perform - some months produce better long-term customers than others.
how_to_read: Each row represents customers who made their first purchase in that month
Repeat Rate shows what percentage became repeat buyers (2+ orders)
Higher repeat rates indicate better quality customer acquisition that month
Avg CLV shows the long-term value of customers in each cohort
Recent cohorts have lower CLV because they have not had time to mature
Color intensity on Repeat Rate indicates performance vs benchmarks
decisions_answered: Which acquisition months produced the highest-quality customers?
Is our repeat purchase rate improving over time?
How long does it take for a cohort to reach full CLV potential?
Should we replicate campaigns from high-performing cohort months?
payload.narration_text:
This cohort grid shows how customer acquisition quality varies by month.

The formulas: Repeat Rate is the percentage of cohort customers who made at least 2 purchases. Average CLV is total lifetime value divided by cohort size.

Reading the grid: Each row is a monthly cohort - all customers who made their first purchase that month. A 50% repeat rate means half of those new customers came back to buy again. Green highlighting means the cohort outperformed benchmarks.

Important context: Recent cohorts will always show lower CLV because they have not had time to accumulate purchases. Compare repeat rates across cohorts to identify which acquisition channels or campaigns produced the best long-term customers.

payload.formula_text: Cell Value = COUNT(customers) WHERE R_Score = row AND F_Score = column
payload.formula_explanation: This 5x5 matrix shows customer density across all combinations of Recency scores (1-5) and Frequency scores (1-5). Each cell represents customers with that specific R-F combination. Darker cells indicate higher customer concentration.
how_to_read: Y-axis shows Recency score (R5 = most recent, R1 = longest ago)
X-axis shows Frequency score (F5 = most frequent, F1 = one-time buyer)
Top-right corner (R5, F5) contains your best customers
Bottom-right (R1, F5) shows high-frequency customers at risk - they used to buy often
Top-left (R5, F1) shows new customers - recent but only one purchase
Darker cells mean more customers concentrated in that R-F combination
decisions_answered: Where are most of our customers concentrated in the R-F matrix?
Do we have many high-frequency customers at risk (R1-R2, F4-F5)?
What portion of customers are one-time buyers vs repeat?
Which R-F combinations should we target for campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap shows your customer distribution across Recency and Frequency scores.

The formula counts customers for each R-F combination, creating a 5x5 matrix. Scores range from 1 (worst) to 5 (best) on each dimension.

Reading the matrix: The Y-axis shows Recency - R5 means they purchased very recently, R1 means a long time ago. The X-axis shows Frequency - F5 means they buy very often, F1 means one-time buyer. Darker cells have more customers.

The top-right corner is your sweet spot - recent and frequent buyers. The bottom-right is concerning - these are customers who used to buy frequently but have gone quiet. The top-left represents your opportunity - new customers who could become frequent buyers with the right nurturing.

payload.formula_text: Segment % = (Segment Customer Count / Total Customers) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: Customers are assigned to segments based on their RFM scores (Recency, Frequency, Monetary). Each segment represents a distinct behavioral pattern. The chart shows the relative size of each segment as a percentage of your total customer base.
how_to_read: Each horizontal bar represents one customer segment (Champions, Loyal, At Risk, etc.)
Bar length shows customer count - longer bars mean larger segments
Champions (best customers) have high R, F, and M scores - recent, frequent, high value
At Risk segments have high historical value but low recent activity
Colors indicate segment health: green for healthy, red/orange for attention-needed
decisions_answered: What is the distribution of customers across behavioral segments?
Which segments contain the most customers?
Are we growing our Champions segment over time?
How many customers need re-engagement campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows how your customers are distributed across RFM segments.

The formula calculates each segment's share of your total customer base. Customers are assigned to segments based on three scores: Recency (how recently they purchased), Frequency (how often they buy), and Monetary value (how much they spend).

Reading this chart: Longer bars mean more customers. Your Champions segment represents your best customers - they buy recently, frequently, and spend a lot. At Risk and Need Attention segments are customers who were once valuable but have gone quiet.

Key questions answered: What does your customer portfolio look like? Are you building Champions or losing them? Which segments need immediate marketing attention? Use this to prioritize campaigns and resource allocation.

payload.formula_text:
Avg CLV = Total Segment Revenue / Segment Customer Count
Churn Risk = AVG(churn_probability) per segment
payload.formula_explanation: This table provides detailed metrics for each segment including customer count, revenue contribution, average CLV, average order frequency, and churn risk. It helps compare segment performance across multiple dimensions.
how_to_read: Segments are sorted by total revenue contribution by default
Compare % Customers vs % Revenue to find high-value segments
Champions may be 14% of customers but 33% of revenue - disproportionate value
Green churn risk values are healthy, red indicates urgent attention needed
Click column headers to sort by different metrics
Click any row to drill into that segments customer list
decisions_answered: Which segments drive the most revenue?
What is the average CLV difference between segments?
Which segments have the highest churn risk?
How does revenue contribution compare to customer count?
payload.narration_text:
This performance table provides detailed metrics for each customer segment.

The key formulas: Average CLV is total segment revenue divided by customer count. Churn risk is the average predicted churn probability for customers in each segment.

Reading this table: Look for disproportionate value - Champions might be 14% of customers but contribute 33% of revenue. That means each Champion is worth more than twice the average customer. Sort by churn risk to identify segments needing immediate retention campaigns.

Questions answered: Which segments drive your business? Where should marketing focus? A segment with high CLV but high churn risk represents both opportunity and threat - these customers are valuable but at risk of leaving.

payload.formula_text: Monthly Segment Size = COUNT(customers) WHERE segment = X AND snapshot_month = Y
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks how segment sizes change over time using monthly snapshots. This reveals whether your customer health is improving (growing Champions, shrinking At Risk) or deteriorating. Helps identify seasonal patterns and campaign impacts.
how_to_read: Each line represents one customer segment tracked over 12 months
Rising Champions line indicates improving customer quality
Rising At Risk line is a warning sign - more customers disengaging
Look for seasonal patterns (spring/fall planting seasons for garden business)
Sharp changes may indicate campaign impacts or market events
Toggle segments on/off in the legend to focus on specific comparisons
decisions_answered: Are our best segments growing or shrinking over time?
Is our At Risk segment getting larger (customer health declining)?
What seasonal patterns affect customer engagement?
Did specific campaigns or events impact segment migration?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks segment sizes over the past 12 months.

The formula counts customers in each segment at monthly intervals, creating a time series for each segment.

Reading the trends: You want to see Champions and Loyal Customers trending upward - that means your customer base is getting healthier. At Risk and Hibernating trending upward is concerning - more customers are disengaging. Look for seasonal patterns too - a garden business might see Champions spike in spring when customers are actively buying.

Key questions: Is customer health improving overall? Are retention efforts working? Did a specific campaign cause segment migrations? Use this to measure the long-term effectiveness of your marketing programs.
